questions.q: Let $K/\mathbb{Q}$ be Galois with group $G$, and let $\mathfrak{P}\subset\mathcal{O}_K$ be an unramified prime above a rational prime $p$. The Frobenius element $\mathrm{Frob}_\mathfrak{P}\in G$ is characterised by:
choices: $\mathrm{Frob}_\mathfrak{P}$ is the identity in $G$ for every unramified $\mathfrak{P}$
questions.explain: The Frobenius is the unique element of the decomposition group whose image in the residue-field Galois group is the $p$-power map $x\mapsto x^p$; equivalently $\mathrm{Frob}_\mathfrak{P}(x)\equiv x^p\pmod{\mathfrak{P}}$.

questions.q: If $\mathfrak{P}$ and $\mathfrak{P}'$ are two unramified primes of $\mathcal{O}_K$ lying over the same rational prime $p$, the Frobenius elements $\mathrm{Frob}_\mathfrak{P}$ and $\mathrm{Frob}_{\mathfrak{P}'}$ are:
questions.explain: Any two primes above $p$ are related by an element of $G$ (Galois acts transitively on primes above $p$), and conjugating the prime conjugates the Frobenius. So $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ is a well-defined conjugacy class in $G$.

questions.q: For $f(x)=x^3-x-1$ (Galois group $S_3$), a prime $p$ with factorisation $\bar f(x)\bmod p=(\deg 2)\cdot(\deg 1)$ has Frobenius of cycle shape a single transposition. What is the order of $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ in $S_3$?
questions.explain: A transposition in $S_3$ has order $2$: applying it twice returns the identity.

hard.q: Let $K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_p)$ for an odd prime $p$, so $\mathrm{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/p)^\times$. For a prime $\ell\ne p$, the Frobenius $\mathrm{Frob}_\ell$ corresponds to which element of $(\mathbb{Z}/p)^\times$?
hard.hint: Frobenius acts by $\ell$-th powers on roots of unity.
hard.explain: The automorphism $\sigma_\ell:\zeta_p\mapsto\zeta_p^\ell$ is the Frobenius at $\ell$, since $\sigma_\ell(\zeta_p)=\zeta_p^\ell\equiv\zeta_p^\ell\pmod{\mathfrak{P}}$ realizes the $\ell$-power Frobenius on the residue field. Under the identification $\mathrm{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/p)^\times$, $\mathrm{Frob}_\ell$ is the class of $\ell\bmod p$.

hard.q: Which of the following is the correct chain of inclusions and quotients linking inertia, decomposition group, and the full Galois group for a prime $\mathfrak{P}$ above an unramified $p$ in $K/\mathbb{Q}$ Galois?
hard.hint: Unramified means $e=1$, so inertia collapses and the cyclic quotient sees Frobenius.
hard.explain: When $p$ is unramified, $e=1$ so $I(\mathfrak{P})=1$. The decomposition group $D(\mathfrak{P})$ is a subgroup of $G$ of order $f$ generated by $\mathrm{Frob}_\mathfrak{P}$, giving $D(\mathfrak{P})\cong\mathbb{Z}/f\mathbb{Z}$. Its cosets are the $r$ distinct primes above $p$: $[G:D]=r$.

hard.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-23})$, the ring of integers is a non-UFD with class number $3$. The prime $p=2$ has $\mathrm{Frob}_2$ of order $3$ in $\mathrm{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})\cong\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}$... but wait: this is a degree-$2$ extension so $|G|=2$. Instead consider the Hilbert class field $H/\mathbb{Q}$ of $K$, which has $\mathrm{Gal}(H/\mathbb{Q})\cong S_3$ and degree $6$. What is the order of $\mathrm{Frob}_2$ in $S_3$ if $2$ has residue degree $f=3$ in $H/\mathbb{Q}$?
hard.hint: Order of Frobenius equals the residue degree $f$ of the prime.
hard.explain: The Frobenius $\mathrm{Frob}_2\in S_3$ generates the decomposition group $D(\mathfrak{P})\cong\mathbb{Z}/f\mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$. An element of order $3$ in $S_3$ is a $3$-cycle, so $\mathrm{ord}(\mathrm{Frob}_2)=3$.

questions.q: Let $K/\mathbb{Q}$ be Galois of degree $n$ and let $p$ be an unramified rational prime with $p\mathcal{O}_K=\mathfrak{P}_1\cdots\mathfrak{P}_r$ and common residue degree $f$. Which relation must hold?
choices: $r$ and $f$ can depend on the chosen prime $\mathfrak{P}_i$
questions.explain: In a Galois extension $G$ acts transitively on primes above $p$, so $r$ and $f$ are the same for every $\mathfrak{P}_i$, and the degree formula $\sum e_i f_i = n$ collapses (with $e_i=1$) to $rf=n$.

questions.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_7)$, $[K:\mathbb{Q}]=6$, a prime $p$ that is inert in $K$ has splitting type $(r,f)=(1,6)$. If instead $p$ splits into $r=3$ primes, what is the common residue degree $f$?
For $f(x)=x^3-x-1$ with Galois group $S_3$ (degree $6$ splitting field), which Frobenius cycle shape on the roots corresponds to $p$ splitting completely in the splitting field?
questions.explain: Completely split means Frobenius is the identity, which acts on the three roots with cycle shape $(1,1,1)$.

hard.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_7)$ with $[K:\mathbb{Q}]=6$ and $\mathrm{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/7)^\times\cong\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$, the prime $p=2$ has residue degree $f$ equal to the multiplicative order of $2$ modulo $7$. Which splitting type $(r,f)$ does $p=2$ have?
hard.hint: Compute the multiplicative order of $2$ in $(\mathbb{Z}/7)^\times$ to get $f$.
hard.explain: The order of $2$ in $(\mathbb{Z}/7)^\times$: $2^1=2,\ 2^2=4,\ 2^3=8\equiv 1\pmod 7$, so $\mathrm{ord}_7(2)=3=f$. Then $r=n/f=6/3=2$, giving splitting type $(r,f)=(2,3)$.

hard.q: Let $K/\mathbb{Q}$ be the splitting field of $x^4-2$ with Galois group $D_4$ of order $8$. A prime $p$ splits into $r=4$ distinct primes in $\mathcal{O}_K$, each with ramification index $e=1$. What is the residue degree $f$ of each prime above $p$?
hard.explain: The degree formula gives $ref=|G|=8$. With $r=4$ and $e=1$, we get $f=8/(4\cdot 1)=2$.

hard.q: True or false: if $p$ is inert in a quadratic field $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d})$, then $p$ remains inert in every extension of $K$. Which is the correct counterexample?
choices: There is no counterexample; inertness propagates to all extensions
$p$ is inert in $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})/\mathbb{Q}$ but can split in the Hilbert class field of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$
$p$ inert in $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d})$ implies $p$ is inert in every number field
$p$ inert in $K$ implies $p$ is totally ramified in every extension of $K$
hard.hint: Inertness can break when you climb into an unramified extension like the class field.
hard.explain: Inertness in a subextension does not force inertness upstairs. For $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$ with class number $2$, a prime inert over $\mathbb{Q}$ can split in the Hilbert class field $H/K$ (an unramified degree-$2$ extension), since splitting in $H/\mathbb{Q}$ is governed by the larger Galois group, not just the quadratic Frobenius.

questions.q: For a prime $\mathfrak{P}$ above $p$ in a Galois extension $K/\mathbb{Q}$ with group $G$, the decomposition group $D(\mathfrak{P})$ is defined as:
choices: the kernel of the reduction map $\mathcal{O}_K\to\mathcal{O}_K/\mathfrak{P}$
questions.explain: By definition $D(\mathfrak{P})$ is the stabiliser of $\mathfrak{P}$ under the $G$-action on primes above $p$; its order is $ef=|G|/r$.

questions.q: In a Galois extension of degree $n=12$ where a prime $p$ has $r=3$ primes above it with ramification index $e=2$ and residue degree $f=2$, what is $|D(\mathfrak{P})|$?
Which statement about inertia is TRUE?
choices: $I(\mathfrak{P})$ is the kernel of $D(\mathfrak{P})\to\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{p^f}/\mathbb{F}_p)$, and is trivial exactly when $p$ is unramified
$I(\mathfrak{P})$ is generated by the Frobenius element
questions.explain: Inertia is by definition the kernel of reduction to the residue-field Galois group, has order $e$, and vanishes precisely at unramified primes; when it does, $D/I\cong\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{p^f}/\mathbb{F}_p)$ is generated by Frobenius.

hard.q: Let $K/\mathbb{Q}$ be Galois with $G=S_4$ (order $24$), and let $\mathfrak{P}$ lie above a prime $p$ with $e=2$, $f=3$, $r=4$. Which of the following correctly describes the structure of $D(\mathfrak{P})$?
hard.hint: Size is $ef$, and inertia sits normally with cyclic residue-field quotient.
hard.explain: $|D(\mathfrak{P})|=ef=2\cdot 3=6$ and $[G:D]=r=24/6=4$. \checkmark. The inertia $I(\mathfrak{P})$ has order $e=2$, is normal in $D$, and $D/I\cong\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{p^3}/\mathbb{F}_p)\cong\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$. So $D$ is a group of order $6$ with a normal $\mathbb{Z}/2$ and quotient $\mathbb{Z}/3$; it is $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ or $S_3$.

hard.q: Which of the following is the correct logical order of deductions to show that for an abelian extension $K/\mathbb{Q}$ the Frobenius conjugacy class is a single element?
choices: Conjugacy class is always a point $\Rightarrow$ abelianness is not needed $\Rightarrow$ the claim holds for all Galois extensions

$G$ abelian $\Rightarrow$ all conjugacy classes are singletons $\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{Frob}_{\mathfrak{P}}$ is independent of the choice of $\mathfrak{P}$ above $p$ $\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ is a well-defined element (not just a class)
$\mathrm{Frob}_{\mathfrak{P}}$ is central in $G$ for any Galois extension $\Rightarrow$ we get a well-defined element
Frobenius is canonical only when $r=1$ (inert prime), regardless of abelianness
hard.hint: Singleton conjugacy classes in abelian groups remove the $\mathfrak{P}$-dependence.
hard.explain: In a non-abelian group different primes $\mathfrak{P}$ above $p$ give conjugate but possibly distinct Frobenius elements, so only a conjugacy class is well-defined. When $G$ is abelian every conjugacy class has size $1$, so $\mathrm{Frob}_\mathfrak{P}=\mathrm{Frob}_{\mathfrak{P}'}$ for all $\mathfrak{P},\mathfrak{P}'$ above $p$, yielding a canonical group element $\mathrm{Frob}_p\in G$.

hard.q: In the Galois closure of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})$ over $\mathbb{Q}$, the Galois group is $S_3$ of order $6$. A prime $p\equiv 1\pmod 3$ and $p\nmid 2$ splits into $r=3$ primes above it in $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})$ (degree $3$). What is the order of $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ in the Galois closure $L/\mathbb{Q}$ (with group $S_3$) if $p$ also splits completely in $L$?
hard.hint: Complete splitting means $r=|G|$ and Frobenius is trivial.
hard.explain: Splitting completely in $L/\mathbb{Q}$ means $\mathrm{Frob}_p=\mathrm{id}$, which has order $1$. Complete splitting in the Galois closure is equivalent to $p$ splitting into $[L:\mathbb{Q}]=6$ distinct primes in $\mathcal{O}_L$.

questions.q: Chebotarev's density theorem says that for a Galois extension $K/\mathbb{Q}$ with group $G$ and a conjugacy class $C\subseteq G$, the density of primes $p$ with $\mathrm{Frob}_p\subset C$ equals:
questions.explain: The natural density of unramified primes whose Frobenius class is $C$ is exactly $|C|/|G|$; every conjugacy class is hit by a positive fraction determined by its size.

questions.q: For $f(x)=x^3-x-1$ with $G=S_3$, Chebotarev predicts the density of primes $p$ that split completely (Frobenius = identity). Express this density as a decimal (to $4$ places).
questions.explain: The identity is one conjugacy class of size $1$ in $|S_3|=6$, giving density $1/6\approx 0.1667$.

questions.q: Same $f$, same $G=S_3$: what fraction of primes have $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ a transposition (cycle shape $(2,1)$)?
questions.explain: Transpositions form a conjugacy class of size $3$ in $S_3$, so density $3/6=1/2$.

hard.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{11})$ with $G=\mathrm{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/11)^\times\cong\mathbb{Z}/10\mathbb{Z}$, Chebotarev predicts the density of primes $p$ with $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ of exact order $5$ in $G$. How many elements of $(\mathbb{Z}/11)^\times$ have order $5$? (Give the count as an integer.)
hard.hint: Elements of exact order $5$ in $\mathbb{Z}/10\mathbb{Z}$ are those coprime-to-$5$-times-$2$.
hard.explain: In $\mathbb{Z}/10\mathbb{Z}$ the elements of order $5$ are $\{2,4,6,8\}$ (those $a$ with $\gcd(a,10)=2$, i.e. $5a\equiv 0\pmod{10}$ but $a\not\equiv 0$). There are $\phi(5)=4$ such elements. By Chebotarev, density $=4/10=2/5$.

hard.q: Chebotarev's theorem implies Dirichlet's theorem on primes in arithmetic progressions as a special case. Which Galois extension witnesses this?
choices: $K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_N)$ for primes $\equiv a\pmod N$, via $\mathrm{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/N)^\times$ and the identification $\mathrm{Frob}_p\leftrightarrow [p]$
$K$ must be a non-abelian extension for Dirichlet's theorem to follow
hard.hint: The Galois group of a cyclotomic field is exactly the unit group modulo $N$.
hard.explain: For the cyclotomic field $K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_N)$, $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ corresponds to $[p]\in(\mathbb{Z}/N)^\times$. The Chebotarev density of primes with $\mathrm{Frob}_p=[a]$ is $1/\phi(N)$, which is exactly Dirichlet's theorem: each reduced residue class modulo $N$ contains a positive density $1/\phi(N)$ of primes.

hard.q: Suppose you want to show that a Galois extension $K/\mathbb{Q}$ with group $G$ is NOT abelian using only Chebotarev. Which observation would suffice?
choices: There exists a conjugacy class $C\subset G$ with $|C|>1$ and positive density of primes having Frobenius in $C$
The density of totally split primes equals $1/|G|$
hard.hint: Nontrivial conjugacy class size larger than one rules out abelianness.
hard.explain: In an abelian group every conjugacy class is a singleton. If Chebotarev detects a class $C$ with $|C|>1$ (i.e. primes $p$ and $p'$ with the same Frobenius class but $\mathrm{Frob}_{\mathfrak{P}}\ne\mathrm{Frob}_{\mathfrak{P}'}$ for different primes above $p$), that demonstrates a non-trivial conjugacy class, hence $G$ is non-abelian.

questions.q: Weak (abelian) reciprocity says that if $K/\mathbb{Q}$ is abelian, the map $p\mapsto\mathrm{Frob}_p$ factors through which group, for some conductor $N$?
questions.explain: The Kronecker–Weber theorem embeds every abelian $K$ into a cyclotomic field $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_N)$, whose Galois group is $(\mathbb{Z}/N)^\times$. So Frobenius depends only on $p\bmod N$.

questions.q: Strong (Langlands-style) reciprocity attaches to a Galois representation $\rho:G\to\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ an automorphic object $\pi_\rho$ matching Frobenius traces. The identity is:
questions.explain: The slogan is: the Frobenius trace on the Galois side equals the $p$-th Fourier coefficient of an automorphic form on the other side, at every unramified prime.

questions.q: For $f(x)=x^3-x-1$ (Galois group $S_3$), the associated weight-1 cusp form $\eta(z)\eta(23z)=\sum a_n q^n$ has $a_p=2$ exactly when $p$ splits completely. For such a splitting prime $p$, what is $\operatorname{tr}\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_p)$ where $\rho$ is the standard $2$-dim representation of $S_3$?
questions.explain: If $\mathrm{Frob}_p=e$ (identity), $\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_p)=I_2$ has trace $2$. This matches $a_p=2$ for the modular form, illustrating $\operatorname{tr}\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_p)=a_p$.

hard.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$ with conductor $N=5$, the Artin map sends $p\mapsto\left(\frac{5}{p}\right)\in\{\pm 1\}\cong\mathrm{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})$. For $p=11$, what is $\mathrm{Frob}_{11}$?
hard.hint: Use quadratic reciprocity to flip $(5/11)$ into something modulo $5$.
hard.explain: $11\equiv 1\pmod 5$, and by the Kronecker–Weber / abelian reciprocity the Legendre symbol $\left(\frac{5}{11}\right)=\left(\frac{11}{5}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)=1$ (quadratic reciprocity + $11\equiv 1\pmod 5$). So $\mathrm{Frob}_{11}=+1$, i.e. $11$ splits in $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$.

hard.q: The Artin $L$-function for a representation $\rho:G\to\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ is defined as $L(s,\rho)=\prod_p\det(I-\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_p)p^{-s})^{-1}$ (product over unramified $p$). If $\rho=\mathbf{1}$ is the trivial representation, $L(s,\mathbf{1})$ equals:
hard.hint: A one-dimensional trivial representation just gives the usual Euler product.
hard.explain: For the trivial representation $\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_p)=I_1=1$, so each Euler factor is $(1-p^{-s})^{-1}$, and the product over all primes is precisely $\zeta(s)=\prod_p(1-p^{-s})^{-1}$.

hard.q: The correct order of logical steps in deriving that $p$ splits completely in an abelian extension $K/\mathbb{Q}$ of conductor $N$ if and only if $p\equiv 1\pmod N$ (for $K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_N)$) is:
choices: Use Chebotarev $\Rightarrow$ define Frobenius $\Rightarrow$ note $\mathrm{Frob}_p=\mathrm{id}\Leftrightarrow p^1\equiv 1\pmod N$ in $(\mathbb{Z}/N)^\times$

Identify $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_N)/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/N)^\times$ via $\sigma_a:\zeta_N\mapsto\zeta_N^a$ $\Rightarrow$ note $\mathrm{Frob}_p=\sigma_p$ $\Rightarrow$ $p$ splits completely iff $\sigma_p=\mathrm{id}$ iff $p\equiv 1\pmod N$
Show Galois group is abelian $\Rightarrow$ all primes split $\Rightarrow$ conclude $p\equiv 1\pmod N$
Apply Dirichlet's theorem directly without any reference to Galois theory
hard.hint: Identify $\sigma_p$ with the class of $p$ in $(\mathbb{Z}/N)^\times$.
hard.explain: Step 1: the standard isomorphism $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_N)/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/N)^\times$ sends $\sigma_a$ to $[a]$. Step 2: for unramified $p\nmid N$, the Frobenius is $\sigma_p$ (since $\sigma_p(\zeta_N)=\zeta_N^p\equiv\zeta_N^p\pmod{\mathfrak{P}}$). Step 3: $p$ splits completely iff $\mathrm{Frob}_p=\mathrm{id}$ iff $\sigma_p=\sigma_1$ iff $p\equiv 1\pmod N$.
